instruction: I have circular dead patches in my Kentucky bluegrass lawn that look like donuts. What could be causing this?
response: What you're describing sounds like necrotic ring spot (NRS), which is the most destructive fungal disease of Kentucky bluegrass here in Colorado. It's caused by the pathogen Ophiosphaerella korrae and produces those characteristic doughnut or frog-eye shaped patches of dead grass, often several inches to several feet across. Symptoms typically intensify in late July and August during summer heat stress, though initial signs can appear in early fall or the following spring. Give us a call at (303) 993-8717 and we can evaluate your lawn and recommend an integrated treatment approach.

instruction: When does necrotic ring spot typically show up in Denver-area lawns?
response: Necrotic ring spot symptoms can appear throughout the growing season, but they commonly intensify during late July and August when your lawn is under summer heat stress. New infections often first show up in early fall or the following spring as light green to straw-colored patches. The disease tends to appear two to three years after the fungus is introduced to a lawn, so it can seem to come out of nowhere. At Denver Sprinkler and Landscape, we can help you identify and manage NRS with proper turf care practices and targeted treatments.

instruction: Can sulfur help with necrotic ring spot in my bluegrass lawn?
response: Yes, sulfur amendments have been shown to reduce necrotic ring spot severity. Sulfur is thought to acidify the soil around the roots and inhibit the NRS pathogen. You can apply sulfur-coated urea during regular fertilization or use elemental sulfur in split applications totaling about 1 to 1.5 pounds per 1,000 square feet per year, with the higher rate providing better control. Be sure to water it into the soil to avoid leaf damage, and monitor your soil pH each year, stopping applications if it drops below 6.0. Our team at Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you develop a fertilization and amendment plan tailored to your lawn's needs.

instruction: How much nitrogen should I apply to my lawn that has necrotic ring spot?
response: When dealing with necrotic ring spot, it's important not to over-fertilize. You should avoid applying more than 1 pound of total nitrogen in a fast-release formulation like urea per 1,000 square feet at any single application during spring and summer. Following a late-season fertilization program helps reduce NRS and other lawn disease problems. Excessive nitrogen actually promotes the conditions that NRS thrives in. If you'd like a customized fertilization plan for your Denver lawn, reach out to us at info@denversprinklerservices.com.

instruction: Should I leave fallen leaves on my lawn over the winter to help pollinators?
response: While leaving leaf litter can benefit pollinators like native bumblebees that hibernate in it over winter, you should avoid leaving leaves directly on your lawn. Leaves left on turf can smother the grass and promote mold and disease growth. Instead, rake or blow leaves into garden beds, under shrubs, or into a designated undisturbed area where they can provide pollinator habitat without harming your lawn. Try to leave that litter in place until at least mid-April to mid-May, when bumblebees typically emerge from hibernation. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you with a fall cleanup plan that balances lawn health with pollinator support.

instruction: What are the benefits of composting my fall leaves instead of sending them to the landfill?
response: Composting your fall leaves is a fantastic eco-friendly choice with real benefits for your Denver landscape. When leaves break down, they feed soil microbes, encourage earthworm activity, and recycle nutrients back into your soil. This enriches your yard naturally over time. Plus, keeping leaves out of landfills reduces waste and saves you the hassle of bagging and hauling. You can mulch leaves with a mower, compost them in a bin, or use them as mulch around trees and garden beds. Contact us at (303) 993-8717 if you'd like help setting up a sustainable fall yard care plan.

instruction: What are some good drought-tolerant perennial flowers I can plant in my Denver yard?
response: Colorado has several great drought-tolerant perennial options for Denver landscapes. Blanket flower (Gaillardia) produces showy daisy-like blooms in orange, red, and yellow and thrives in xeric conditions. Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) varieties like Hidcote and Munstead offer aromatic blooms and semi-evergreen foliage with low water needs. Red-hot poker (Kniphofia uvaria) sends up dramatic spikes of orange, red, and yellow flowers from clumps of grass-like foliage. Sun rose (Helianthemum) forms low-growing, spreading mounds that are perfect for rock gardens. Our team at Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you design a beautiful, water-wise perennial garden with an efficient drip irrigation system.

instruction: Is lavender a good choice for my Colorado garden?
response: Absolutely, lavender can do well in Colorado gardens. English lavender (Lavandula angustifolia), particularly the Hidcote and Munstead varieties, is well-suited to our climate. It produces aromatic blooms, has semi-evergreen foliage, and is quite drought-tolerant once established. Lavender prefers well-drained soil and full sun, so make sure to plant it in a spot that doesn't stay soggy. It's marked as suitable for xeric conditions, making it a great fit for Denver's semi-arid environment. If you'd like help incorporating lavender or other low-water perennials into your landscape, give Denver Sprinkler and Landscape a call at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What are some good ground-cover perennials for my Denver landscape?
response: For ground cover in the Denver area, creeping phlox (Phlox subulata) is an excellent choice, forming a carpet-like mat of blooms in early spring with needle-like foliage that stays low. Varieties like Candy Stripe and Emerald Cushion Blue are popular options. Creeping baby's breath (Gypsophila repens) works well in rock gardens and on banks. For shady spots, creeping phlox (Phlox stolonifera) is shade-tolerant and comes in blue, pink, and purple. These perennials help reduce erosion, suppress weeds, and add year-round visual interest to your landscape. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape to discuss the best ground cover options for your yard's specific conditions.

instruction: What can I do year-round to help pollinators in my Denver yard?
response: Helping pollinators goes well beyond just skipping mowing in May. Research shows that creating dedicated pollinator habitat has a proven positive impact on bee populations. You can plant native, low-water plants that provide nectar and pollen throughout the growing season, and leave some undisturbed leaf litter areas where native bumblebees can hibernate over winter. Attracting native bees to your landscape with diverse plantings and avoiding excessive pesticide use also makes a big difference. At Denver Sprinkler and Landscape, we can help you design a pollinator-friendly landscape that's also beautiful and water-efficient. Call us at (303) 993-8717 to get started.

instruction: Does reducing mowing really help bees and other pollinators?
response: Yes, research supports the connection between mowing frequency and pollinator health. Studies published in Biological Conservation have shown that mowing less frequently increases bee abundance and diversity in suburban yards. A meta-analysis in Urban Forestry & Urban Greening also confirmed that reduced mowing positively impacts arthropod abundance and diversity in urban settings. However, the benefits extend beyond just mowing less. Creating dedicated pollinator habitat with native plants, leaving undisturbed areas for hibernation, and reducing pesticide use all contribute to healthier pollinator populations in your Denver yard. Our team can help you find the right balance between a well-maintained lawn and pollinator support.

instruction: How do I winterize the backflow preventer on my sprinkler system?
response: Winterizing your backflow preventer depends on the type you have. If you have a Pressure Vacuum Breaker (PVB) or Reduced Pressure (RP) device, never blow it out with compressed air, as the rubber seals inside can melt from the heat. Instead, after the system is blown out, exercise both ball valves and test cocks back and forth several times, then leave them in a half-open position for the winter. Ball valves left fully open or fully closed can trap water, which expands when it freezes and causes damage. For Atmospheric Vacuum Breakers (AVBs), they are winterized with compressed air when the zone is blown out. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape offers professional winterization services to protect your entire system. Call us at (303) 993-8717 to schedule.

instruction: Do I need to drain the pump on my irrigation system before winter?
response: If your sprinkler system has a pump, it should absolutely be drained before winter. Ideally, you should drain it, remove it from the system, and store it inside where it's protected from freezing temperatures. If removing the pump isn't possible, drain it thoroughly and wrap it with insulation to protect it from the weather. Frozen water inside a pump can cause serious and expensive damage. Our team at Denver Sprinkler and Landscape handles sprinkler winterization throughout the Denver metro area, and we make sure every component, including pumps and backflow devices, is properly protected. Reach out at (303) 993-8717 to schedule your winterization appointment.

instruction: What type of strawberry plants grow best in the Denver area?
response: For the Denver area, everbearing strawberry varieties like Ogallala and Fort Laramie are highly recommended because they're more cold-hardy and reliable producers in Colorado's climate. If a late spring frost kills the first flowers, you'll still get a crop in late summer or fall. June-bearing varieties like Guardian, Kent, and Honeoye produce the most flavorful berries but ripen in July and risk losing their entire crop to a late frost. Day-neutral varieties like Tribute and Tristar fruit more consistently throughout summer. Many gardeners plant all three types to extend their harvest season. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help set up an efficient drip irrigation system for your strawberry beds.

instruction: When do strawberries ripen in Colorado?
response: In most areas of Colorado, June-bearing strawberry varieties actually ripen in July despite their name. Everbearing types provide two main crops, one in early summer and a lighter one in late summer or early fall, with small amounts of fruit in between. Day-neutral varieties produce fruit more consistently throughout the entire summer growing season. To extend your harvest, consider planting a mix of all three types. Proper irrigation is key to a good strawberry crop in Colorado's dry climate. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape at (303) 993-8717 if you need help setting up efficient watering for your garden beds.

instruction: How do I protect my strawberry plants through a Denver winter?
response: To protect strawberry plants through our Denver winters, apply straw mulch or row cover fabric after the initial freezes, ideally by December 1st or earlier. Distribute the mulch over the plants to a depth of 1 to 2 inches. This prevents the plants from losing moisture to our drying winter winds and protects roots from damage caused by the freeze-thaw cycles common in Colorado. In spring, leave the mulch on as long as possible to delay early growth that could be killed by late frosts. Check under the mulch in March for new growth, and gradually part the mulch to let sunlight reach the foliage as the season progresses. You can rake it back over the plants if a late frost threatens.

instruction: When is the best time to plant ornamental grasses in Denver?
response: The best time to plant ornamental grasses in Denver is in spring through early fall, but avoid planting late in the season, especially warm-season grass types. Late planting doesn't give them enough time to establish roots before winter. Most ornamental grasses need at least three to five hours of direct sun each day. In too much shade, they may not bloom, tend to flop over, and won't develop their signature fall color. Before new growth starts in spring, cut them back and apply mulch. Use hedge shears and wear gloves, as some species have razor-sharp leaf edges. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you select and install the right grasses for your landscape.

instruction: What ornamental grasses are native to Colorado and do well in dry conditions?
response: Several excellent native ornamental grasses thrive in Colorado's dry conditions. Indian rice grass (Achnatherum hymenoides) forms tufted clumps with airy flower panicles and grows well in sandy, well-drained soils, though it goes dormant with summer heat. Big bluestem (Andropogon gerardii) is a native with striking blue-green foliage that turns orange in fall, featuring three-branched seed heads that resemble bird's feet. It tolerates clay but performs best in sandy soil. Both are well-suited to Denver's climate and can add beautiful texture and movement to xeric landscapes. Call Denver Sprinkler and Landscape at (303) 993-8717 for help designing a low-water landscape with native grasses.

instruction: Can I grow pampas grass in Denver?
response: Traditional pampas grass (Cortaderia selloana) is not reliably hardy in Denver's climate. However, there are two alternatives worth considering. Dwarf pampas grass (Cortaderia selloana 'Pumila') is a more cold-hardy version that may survive as a semi-perennial once established in the warmest parts of USDA Zone 5, producing fluffy white flower heads. An even more reliable choice is plume grass (Saccharum ravennae), which serves as the northern substitute for pampas grass and produces fluffy, cream-colored flowers on 8-foot stalks in August. Switch grass (Panicum virgatum) is another great native alternative with showy flowers and yellow fall color. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape for help selecting ornamental grasses that thrive in our area.

instruction: How can I tell if my tree has root disease?
response: There are several warning signs that your tree may have root problems. Look for small, yellowing or chlorotic foliage, reduced overall growth, leaves tufted only at branch tips, and progressive branch dieback starting in the upper crown. More advanced signs include fungal fruiting bodies like mushrooms or conks appearing at the tree's base, and white fungal growth under the bark. Keep in mind that symptoms from root damage, such as from construction or soil compaction, may not appear for one to several years after the damage occurred. If you suspect root disease, you can carefully dig at a root and remove a small patch of bark. Brown coloration underneath indicates a dead root, while healthy roots appear white or light-colored.

instruction: What's the best way to water my trees to keep the roots healthy?
response: For healthy tree roots, long, deep watering over the entire root system is far better than frequent light watering. Make sure to allow the soil to dry between waterings. A healthy root environment needs adequate growing space, well-conditioned soil 16 to 24 inches deep, and sufficient water and oxygen. To check your soil condition, dig a hole outside the tree's dripline. If you can't get the shovel in the ground, the soil is too dry. Soil moisture is adequate if the soil can be formed into a ball with light pressure. During extended dry spells, monthly watering is important, especially through Denver's dry winters. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you set up proper tree watering zones in your irrigation system. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What kind of irrigation is safest for my vegetable garden?
response: For food safety in your vegetable garden, soaker hoses or drip irrigation are the best choices. These methods minimize contact between water and the edible parts of your plants, which reduces the potential for contamination from soil-borne pathogens. You should also always use potable water (equal in quality to tap water) when watering your garden and when rinsing produce after harvest. If you're using well water, have it tested every one to two years and more frequently if the well is in poor condition. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape specializes in drip irrigation system installation for vegetable gardens. Give us a call at (303) 993-8717 to discuss your setup.

instruction: Is it safe to use manure in my vegetable garden?
response: You need to be careful with manure in vegetable gardens. Raw, uncomposted manure can harbor disease-causing bacteria that survive for a year or longer. Any manure or similar materials must be composted to proper temperatures first to kill harmful bacteria before applying them to food gardens. After handling manure, be sure to thoroughly wash any tools and gloves that came in contact with it before using them in your garden. Using potable water for rinsing harvested produce is also essential. If you'd like help setting up a safe, efficient irrigation system for your vegetable garden, contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape at info@denversprinklerservices.com.

instruction: What should I consider before planting a large shade tree in my Denver yard?
response: Before planting a large shade tree, consider several key factors. First, make sure you have enough space for the tree's mature size without requiring severe pruning, and check for obstructions from buildings, overhead utility lines, and fences. If lateral space is limited, choose a tree with a narrow, upright growth habit. Second, prepare the soil properly by digging a saucer-shaped hole at least twice the width of the root ball, and make sure the first root flare is at or one inch above soil grade since planting too deeply causes long-term problems. Third, if you have an underground irrigation system, plant trees to allow room for root growth without damaging your lines. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can advise on tree placement that works with your sprinkler system. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Do shade trees really help with energy costs and air quality?
response: Shade trees provide significant practical benefits beyond their beauty. They help moderate temperature extremes in your home, reduce the impact of harsh winter winds, and can offset poor air quality in urban areas like Denver. A well-placed deciduous tree provides cooling shade in summer and allows sunlight through in winter after it drops its leaves. Trees that are prone to storm damage should be planted away from buildings, walkways, and driveways to minimize risk. Choosing the right species for your specific soil conditions and available space is essential for long-term success. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you select and properly irrigate shade trees for maximum benefit to your property.

instruction: What are some good large deciduous trees for Denver landscapes?
response: Denver offers several excellent options for large deciduous trees. Red oak (Quercus rubra) provides beautiful maroon-red fall color, though you should avoid very alkaline soils. American linden (Tilia americana) features large heart-shaped leaves and fragrant early-summer flowers with lovely yellow fall color. Littleleaf linden (Tilia cordata), especially the Greenspire variety, has a neat formal appearance with glossy dark green leaves and cinnamon-colored bark. For a unique option, Japanese pagodatree (Styphnolobium japonicum) produces creamy midsummer flowers. Be aware that some trees like willows have brittle wood and are best planted near water features away from structures. Our team at Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you choose the right tree and set up proper irrigation for it.

instruction: Do evergreen trees need extra water during Denver's dry winters?
response: Yes, all evergreen trees in the Denver area, whether recently transplanted or well-established, benefit from supplemental watering during winter dry spells. Our winter dry spells are often accompanied by drying winds and unseasonably warm temperatures, which increases moisture loss through the needles. Since evergreens keep their foliage year-round, they continue losing water to transpiration even in winter. Watering on warm days when the ground isn't frozen helps prevent winter desiccation damage. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you plan a winter watering schedule for your evergreens. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What are some recommended evergreen trees for Colorado?
response: Colorado has many great evergreen tree options. White fir (Abies concolor) is a beautiful choice with its blue-green needles. Rocky Mountain juniper (Juniperus scopulorum) comes in several excellent varieties including Cologreen, Gray Gleam, Skyrocket, and Wichita Blue, all well-adapted to our climate. Eastern Redcedar (Juniperus virginiana), particularly the Blue Arrow variety, offers a narrow columnar form. For higher elevations above 6,000 feet, some species may not be adapted, so selection is important. Proper planting technique is critical for all evergreens, as planting too deep leads to trunk-girdling roots that can significantly shorten the tree's lifespan. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape to discuss the best evergreen options for your property.

instruction: What are some large deciduous shrubs that work well in Denver landscapes?
response: Several large deciduous shrubs (over 6 feet tall at maturity) perform well in Denver. Serviceberry (Amelanchier spp.) works as a shrub or small tree with attractive berries. Redtwig dogwood (Cornus sericea) provides stunning red stems for winter interest, and the Flaviramea variety offers yellow winter stems instead. Smokebush (Cotinus coggygria) produces unique flowers that look like pink smoke, with purple-leaved varieties like Royal Purple adding dramatic color. PeeGee hydrangea (Hydrangea paniculata 'Grandiflora') puts on a show with its large flower clusters. For hedging, Cheyenne privet is a solid choice. Our team at Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you select and properly irrigate shrubs suited to your specific landscape conditions.

instruction: What shrub has colorful stems for winter interest in my yard?
response: Redtwig dogwood (Cornus sericea) is one of the best shrubs for winter interest in Denver landscapes. Its bright red stems really stand out against snow and winter landscapes. If you prefer a different color, the Flaviramea variety produces beautiful yellow stems in winter instead. Both are large shrubs that are well-adapted to Colorado's climate. Forsythia varieties like Meadowlark, Northern Gold, and Northern Sun are also great choices, specifically bred for hardy flower buds that survive our cold winters and provide early spring color. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help incorporate winter-interest plants into your landscape design. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How should I fertilize my squash and cucumber plants?
response: For vine crops like squash, cucumbers, melons, and pumpkins, you should apply a side-dressing of 2.5 ounces of nitrogen for each 250 feet of row when the plants begin to vine in midseason. Side-dressing means applying the fertilizer beside the crop row rather than directly on the plants. One important caution: never use 'Weed and Feed' type fertilizers on your vegetable garden, as they contain weed killers that will kill your vegetable plants. If you need help setting up an efficient drip irrigation system for your vegetable garden, Denver Sprinkler and Landscape is here to help. Contact us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Can I use weed and feed on my vegetable garden?
response: No, you should never use 'Weed and Feed' type fertilizers on vegetables. These products contain weed killers (herbicides) that will damage or kill your vegetable plants. Instead, use straight nitrogen fertilizers appropriate for each type of vegetable. For vine crops like squash and cucumbers, side-dress with nitrogen when plants begin to vine. For tomatoes and peppers, apply nitrogen at planting and again at specific growth stages. Also, urea or ammonium nitrate fertilizers are not recommended nitrogen sources for some vegetable crops. If you have questions about keeping your garden healthy and well-watered, reach out to Denver Sprinkler and Landscape at info@denversprinklerservices.com.

instruction: What are fire-resistant plants I can use near my Denver home?
response: While no plant is completely fireproof, choosing low-flammability plants and placing them correctly can help protect your home. The Colorado State Forest Service recommends creating defensible space zones around your property. Zone 1 extends 0 to 5 feet from your home and Zone 2 covers 5 to 30 feet. For these closest zones, only plants rated 8, 9, or 10 on a flammability scale of 0 to 10 should be used. Defensible space is about increasing both horizontal and vertical spacing between plants and reducing overall plant volume. If you live in a wildfire-prone area near Denver or in the foothills, contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape at (303) 993-8717 to discuss fire-resistant landscaping options.

instruction: What are defensible space zones and why do they matter for my landscaping?
response: Defensible space zones are critical areas around your home designed to help mitigate fire risk. Zone 1 extends 0 to 5 feet from your structure, Zone 2 covers 5 to 30 feet, and Zone 3 extends from 30 to 100 feet. In Zones 1 and 2, closest to your home, you should only use low-flammability plants rated 8 or higher on a scale of 0 to 10. The key principles are increasing spacing between plants (both horizontally and vertically) and reducing plant volume to slow fire spread. For a personalized defensible space plan, the Colorado State Forest Service recommends contacting your local CSU Extension office. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help implement fire-resistant landscaping with appropriate irrigation for your property.

instruction: How do I start composting my yard waste in Denver?
response: Starting a compost pile is straightforward. Alternate different types of shredded plant materials in 6- to 8-inch layers, using equal parts by volume of dry (brown) and green plant materials. This layering helps your compost reach the correct nitrogen balance for efficient decomposition. Composting reduces yard waste volume by 50 to 75 percent and recycles nutrients back into your yard. Keep in mind that compost is a soil amendment rather than a fertilizer, so it improves soil structure but contains limited plant nutrients. It improves drainage and aeration in clay soils and helps sandy soils hold water and nutrients. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you use finished compost effectively in your landscape. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Is it safe to use compost made with manure in my vegetable garden?
response: You should use caution with manure-based compost in food gardens. Newer strains of bacteria that cause food-borne illnesses have made compost made with manure questionable for use around edible plants. If you do use manure, it must be composted to proper temperatures to kill harmful bacteria, as disease-causing organisms can survive in raw manure for a year or longer. For food gardens, consider composting only yard waste materials instead. The finished compost will still improve your soil by increasing drainage in clay, helping sandy soil hold moisture, and boosting beneficial earthworm and microorganism activity. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape for garden irrigation advice at info@denversprinklerservices.com.

instruction: How big should my compost bin be?
response: For efficient composting, your pile or bin needs to be large enough to build up and retain heat, generally around 36 inches by 36 inches by 36 inches (about 3 feet cubed). Well-insulated wood or plastic structures can be somewhat smaller and still maintain adequate composting temperatures. If you put the compost together correctly, it should heat up within two days. Wire bins can have drying problems, especially on windy sides, so you may need plastic covers or tarps. For small yards, compact plastic composters from garden centers work well, and some use a vertical system where fresh materials go in the top and finished compost comes out the bottom. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape is happy to help with landscape advice at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How can I reduce my water bill by changing my Denver landscape?
response: The average home landscape uses as much as 50 percent of household water, so there's significant savings potential. Start by surveying your yard for turf areas that are hard to water and maintain, such as along fences, on slopes where water runs off, corners where sprinklers overshoot, and narrow mowing strips. Convert these areas to low-water ground covers instead of thirsty grass. Update your irrigation controller for cycle-and-soak watering to minimize runoff, and modify sprinkler heads to water only turf, not hardscapes like sidewalks and driveways. Adding a low-pressure drip irrigation system for garden beds saves even more water. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape specializes in these water-saving retrofits. Call us at (303) 993-8717 for a consultation.

instruction: Should I keep any lawn at all if I'm xeriscaping my Denver yard?
response: Yes, turf still plays a valuable role in a xeriscape when well-placed and maintained. Lawn prevents erosion from wind and water, filters runoff, builds soil health, and provides fire control in urban settings. Play areas for children and exercise areas for pets are often best kept as turfgrass that handles heavy wear. The key is to remove turf from areas where it's difficult to water and maintain, like steep slopes, fence lines, corners where sprinklers overshoot, and narrow strips. Keep lawn where you actually use and enjoy it, and convert the rest to low-water plantings. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you redesign your irrigation system to match your new landscape layout.

instruction: What low-water ground covers work on sunny slopes in Denver?
response: Several excellent spreading plants thrive on hot, sunny slopes in Denver with minimal water. Native options include common yarrow (Achillea millefolium), buffalo grass (Bouteloua dactyloides), blue grama grass (Bouteloua gracilis), and prairie winecups (Callirhoe involucrata). Rocky Mountain penstemon (Penstemon strictus) and creeping penstemon (Penstemon caespitosus) are also great native choices. Ice plant (Delosperma species), creeping juniper (Juniperus horizontalis), and various sedum species round out the options. Many of these are also great for erosion control on slopes. Don't forget that even xeric plants need more water during their first couple of growing seasons while they establish. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can set up temporary drip irrigation for establishment.

instruction: How do I choose the right xeric trees and shrubs for my Denver yard?
response: Selecting xeric trees and shrubs requires careful evaluation of your site before planting. Assess your soil type, drainage conditions, and exposure to heat and wind. Since woody plants are a long-term investment, this upfront assessment saves money and disappointment. While many xeric plants tolerate reduced water, some struggle in Denver's dense clay soils that have minimal room for both water and oxygen around roots. Adding organic amendments before planting can help overcome this issue during establishment. Also, plant xeric trees and shrubs in separate zones from your lawn unless you're using a low-water turf type. Regardless of how drought-tolerant a plant is, most need at least two growing seasons to establish, with gradually reduced watering after that. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape at (303) 993-8717 for help planning your xeric landscape.

instruction: How long do I need to water new xeric plants before they're established?
response: Even drought-tolerant xeric plants need at least two full growing seasons of supplemental watering to properly establish their root systems. During this establishment period, apply adequate water to encourage deep root growth. After the second season, gradually reduce irrigation rather than cutting it off abruptly. Some plants will actually need more water as they get larger, while others need less, so check on them frequently and adjust accordingly. It's also important not to just set and forget your irrigation timer, since watering needs change over the growing season and as plants mature. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can design a drip irrigation system with adjustable zones that makes this transition easy to manage. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What are some water-wise shrubs that do well in Denver?
response: Denver has many great xeric shrub options. Apache plume (Fallugia paradoxa) produces white flowers from May through August with attractive rose-colored feathery seed heads. New Mexican privet (Forestiera neomexicana) has a nice rounded form with erect arching branches and blue-black fruit. Rock spirea (Holodiscus dumosus) features fine-textured foliage that turns red in fall with white flowers in May and June. Sand cherry (Prunus besseyi) offers white spring flowers, purplish-black fruit, and red fall color. Cliff fendlerbush (Fendlera rupicola) provides fragrant flowers and interesting bark texture. For evergreen options, junipers and mugo pines come in many water-wise forms and sizes. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you design a beautiful, low-water shrub border with proper irrigation.

instruction: Should I treat my ash tree for lilac/ash borer?
response: Mature, healthy ash trees usually don't need treatment for lilac/ash borer. Since this pest prefers to infest trees under stress, the best long-term strategy is to address whatever's causing the stress, particularly inadequate watering. However, high-risk trees like recently transplanted ash, those in poor sites, or trees with limited water access may benefit from treatment. One important consideration in Colorado right now is emerald ash borer (EAB), which is deadly to all ash trees. Since only healthy trees are candidates for EAB insecticide treatment, maintaining your ash tree's vigor is doubly important. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help ensure your trees are properly irrigated to stay healthy and resilient. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: My ash tree looks stressed and has holes in the bark. What should I do?
response: Holes in the bark of a stressed ash tree could indicate lilac/ash borer damage. These borers produce tunnels that can extend 7 to 32 centimeters under the bark, and the adults push through paper-thin bark coverings when they emerge. Since this pest targets stressed trees, the priority is addressing the underlying stress. Supplemental watering is often the most important step, as water stress is a common trigger for borer infestation. However, you should also consider whether emerald ash borer (EAB) could be involved, as EAB is now present in Colorado and is fatal to all ash trees. Contact your local county Extension office for identification help, and reach out to Denver Sprinkler and Landscape at (303) 993-8717 to ensure your trees are getting adequate irrigation.

instruction: We're planning a home addition. How do we protect the trees in our yard during construction?
response: Protecting your trees during construction starts with planning before any work begins. Have a certified arborist or city forester evaluate every tree in the construction area to determine if preservation is worthwhile, considering each tree's location, health, size, species, vigor, and the cost of preservation versus removal. Many Colorado towns have tree protection specifications in their land use codes that you must follow. The biggest threats to trees during construction are soil compaction and grade changes, which damage root health and can lead to branch dieback or even tree death. Establish specific guidelines with your contractor and install properly placed barricades and fencing to keep heavy equipment away from tree root zones. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help assess your irrigation system's tree zones before and after construction. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Can adding soil over tree roots during a construction project harm my trees?
response: Yes, adding excessive fill soil over tree roots can seriously harm or kill your trees, though symptoms may not appear for several years. Adding topsoil of less than 3 inches generally won't harm most trees, but the fill should have good drainage properties and not be clay. For deeper fills, an air supply system like a drywell is needed to get oxygen to the roots. Early signs of decline from too much fill include smaller leaf size, leaf scorch, premature fall coloring, and progressive twig dieback in the upper crown. Similarly, lowering the grade and cutting through major roots can cause decline, instability, or death. If grade changes are necessary near trees, terracing or retaining walls that extend to at least the tree's dripline can help protect them.

instruction: My tree started losing branches after we had construction done last year. Is that related?
response: It very likely is related. Trees with roots injured during construction often show branch dieback within a few months of the initial injury, but sometimes symptoms take several years to appear depending on the species and the tree's overall health before the damage. Soil compaction from heavy equipment, grade changes, and root severing are common construction impacts. The best approach now is to prune dying branches to prevent insect and disease damage from spreading to the rest of the tree, but wait to see the full extent of dieback before doing major pruning. Supporting the tree with proper deep watering over the entire root system can help it recover. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can set up a supplemental watering plan for your stressed trees. Contact us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Why do I have dry spots in my lawn even though my sprinklers are running?
response: Dry spots are one of the most common sprinkler issues we see in Denver lawns, and there are several likely causes. Tilted sprinkler heads are very common due to strikes from mowers and foot traffic, and they warp the spray pattern, throwing more water to one side and less to the other. Another frequent issue is mixing different head types on the same zone. For example, rotor heads deliver only one-third the water of spray heads in the same run time, so if both are on one zone, some areas get too much water while others get too little. Over-pressurized heads create mist that blows away in our Denver winds, wasting water. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can perform a full system audit to identify and fix these issues. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How do I know if my sprinkler system water pressure is too high or too low?
response: There are clear signs for both pressure problems. Over-pressurized sprinkler heads emit a fine mist instead of defined streams or a fan pattern, and that mist blows away in the wind, wasting water. An in-line pressure regulator can solve this if your water pressure is too high. Low pressure causes large water droplets that fall short of the intended coverage area, leaving dry spots. For low pressure, you may need different heads or nozzles that require less pressure, or in severe cases, a booster pump. All heads on each zone should be the same type from the same manufacturer with matching precipitation rates. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape specializes in diagnosing and fixing pressure issues. Reach out to us at (303) 993-8717 or info@denversprinklerservices.com.

instruction: How do I calculate how long to run each sprinkler zone?
response: You can determine the right run time using a simple can test. Place four identical, straight-sided cans between the sprinkler heads in one zone. Run the zone for 15 minutes, then pour the water from three cans into the fourth. Measure the depth in inches in that fourth can. Since each can represents 15 minutes (one-quarter hour), the total water in the fourth can equals the precipitation rate in inches per hour for that zone. To calculate run time, divide the amount of water you want to apply (in inches) by the precipitation rate, then multiply by 60 minutes. For example, if your precipitation rate is 1.5 inches per hour and you need 0.5 inches, your run time would be 20 minutes. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can perform this analysis for your entire system. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Should I group plants with similar water needs together in my landscape?
response: Absolutely, grouping plants with similar water requirements together, known as hydrozoning, is one of the most effective ways to save water and keep all your plants healthy. An established border of medium to low water-using shrubs requires significantly less irrigation than bluegrass turf or a vegetable garden, so they should be on separate zones. This way, you can set different run times on your controller for each zone based on the actual needs of those plants. Factors like sun exposure, wind, slope, and soil type also affect water needs within your landscape. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape designs and installs irrigation systems with proper hydrozoning to maximize efficiency and plant health. Contact us at (303) 993-8717 for a consultation.

instruction: How often should I water a new perennial garden in Denver?
response: New perennial plantings in full sun often need water every couple of days during the first month after planting, depending on weather conditions. After that first month, gradually extend the interval to four to six days between watering to encourage roots to expand into the surrounding soil. Mulched plants won't dry out as quickly, so applying mulch helps reduce watering frequency. Watch for signs that irrigation needs to increase, such as drooping leaves or stems and blossom drop. Keep in mind that perennial gardens have different water needs than turf, so plan to water them separately, ideally with a drip irrigation system. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can install efficient drip irrigation for your perennial beds. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How should I arrange perennials on a sloped yard?
response: When planting perennials on slopes, use a hydrozoning approach by grouping plants according to their water needs based on the landscape. Place drought-tolerant plants together at the top of the slope where water drains away quickly, and position moisture-loving plants in swales or low spots where runoff naturally collects. This takes advantage of natural water movement and reduces waste. When selecting plants, also plan for ongoing maintenance needs like pruning, fertilizing, deadheading, and division. Choose varieties with good pest and disease resistance to reduce maintenance on hard-to-reach slope areas. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can design an irrigation system that accounts for your yard's topography. Contact us at info@denversprinklerservices.com.

instruction: When should I mulch my fall-planted bulbs in Denver?
response: After the ground freezes in fall, cover your bulb bed with a 3-inch layer of mulch. This prevents the alternate freezing and thawing cycles that break roots and damage bulbs, which is especially important in Denver's variable winter weather. You can remove the mulch in April before shoots emerge, or leave it in place if the shoots can push through it easily. In spring, leave the mulch on as long as possible to delay early growth, since early spring flowers are vulnerable to our late frosts. Check under the mulch in March for new growth, and when you see it starting, gradually part the mulch to let sunlight reach the foliage. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help with your seasonal landscape maintenance needs. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How do I care for my spring bulbs after they finish blooming?
response: After your bulbs finish blooming, remove the spent flowers right away. The developing seeds will divert food produced by the foliage that should instead be going back into the bulb for next year's blooms. Apply nitrogen fertilizer at 1/4 pound per 100 square feet before the foliage withers, as any fertilizer applied after the bulb goes dormant is wasted. Let the foliage die back naturally since it's feeding the bulb. After the second year, bulbs may start to crowd and lose vigor, producing smaller blooms. When this happens, dig them up in late August, let them dry for a few days in a cool, shady spot, divide them, and replant only the best ones, preferably in a new location. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape for help with seasonal garden care.

instruction: What flowers grow well at high elevations near Denver?
response: Several perennials thrive at higher elevations in Colorado's mountain communities. Yarrow (Achillea species) is easy to grow with aromatic fern-like foliage and long bloom times, and it's deer and rabbit resistant. Monkshood (Aconitum species) produces distinctive hood-shaped purple or pink blossoms and is resistant to pests and diseases, though it is toxic so use caution around children and pets. Ornamental onions (Allium species) are easy to grow and come in purple, white, blue, pink, and yellow. For soil improvement at elevation, add organic matter gradually each year rather than all at once to avoid salt buildup. Snow cover actually helps as a natural mulch, allowing root growth even during winter. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape serves the Denver metro and surrounding foothill communities. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What are good deer-resistant perennials for my mountain property near Denver?
response: Several beautiful perennials are deer and rabbit resistant and well-suited to mountain properties near Denver. Lupines (Lupinus species) produce attractive spikes of pea-like flowers and work beautifully in borders and meadow plantings, with the Russell Hybrids offering a wide variety of colors. Maltese cross (Lychnis chalcedonica) provides bold scarlet color and attracts hummingbirds. Rose campion (Lychnis coronaria) features silvery gray woolly foliage with fuchsia or white blossoms and reseeds readily. Sea lavender (Limonium latifolium) produces delicate baby's breath-like flowers from leathery basal leaves and works great as a cut or dried flower. All of these prefer moderately organic, well-drained soils. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help with irrigation planning for mountain properties.

instruction: Can road deicing salt damage my trees near the street?
response: Yes, magnesium chloride (MgCl2) used for deicing roads and sidewalks can definitely damage your trees. Trees take up chloride and magnesium through their roots from soil that has been contaminated by runoff from treated roads. While both are essential nutrients in small amounts, excessive concentrations become toxic. Chloride is particularly damaging to foliage. High concentrations of MgCl2 in the soil can also disrupt water uptake, making it difficult for trees to absorb water and nutrients even when moisture is present. This is especially concerning for trees planted along Denver's streets and driveways. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help ensure your roadside trees receive adequate deep watering to flush salts through the soil. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Is the deicer I use on my driveway harming my landscape plants?
response: It could be, especially if you're using magnesium chloride (MgCl2) based products, which are very common in Colorado. Granulated MgCl2 applied to sidewalks, driveways, and walkways can move into adjacent soils through precipitation and snowmelt. Once in the soil, chloride ions are taken up by plant roots and accumulate in leaves, where high concentrations cause foliar damage. To protect your landscape plants, consider using non-chloride based deicing products near planted areas. Also be aware that liquid MgCl2 sprayed on nearby roads can splash onto your property. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape recommends deep watering trees and shrubs near treated surfaces to help leach salts below the root zone. Contact us at info@denversprinklerservices.com for more advice.

instruction: How can I protect my trees from road salt damage?
response: There are several strategies to protect your trees from magnesium chloride damage. First, use non-chloride based products for deicing your own sidewalks and driveway, especially near trees. Second, reduce your trees' exposure to road splash by installing barriers like straw, fencing, or other structures between the road and your trees. While the effectiveness of washing salt deposits off needles isn't fully proven, it may help maintain healthy foliage. Additionally, provide supplemental deep watering to help leach accumulated salts below the root zone. Unfortunately, no chemical treatment can fully reclaim saline soils, but proper drainage and flushing with water can remove salt ions from upper soil layers. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can set up deep-watering zones for your roadside trees. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Parts of my lawn are turning straw-colored very quickly. Could it be mites?
response: Rapid browning and bleaching of turf can be caused by Banks grass mite, which is the most destructive turf-damaging mite in Colorado. This mite causes grass to turn a bleached straw color and can kill it rapidly, especially during hot weather. Early signs include small white flecking (stippling) on grass blades and sometimes a slight purpling of injured blades. Banks grass mite damage is almost always associated with drought stress, so maintaining proper irrigation is your best defense. The mite is green during most of the season but may turn bright red when stressed. It's a difficult pest to control with pesticides alone. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help ensure your lawn's irrigation system is keeping up with your turf's water needs, which is the most effective prevention. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How do I control Banks grass mite in my Denver lawn?
response: Banks grass mite is a challenging pest to control, and the most important step is addressing drought stress, since almost all serious damage from this mite is related to insufficient water. Make sure your irrigation system is delivering adequate coverage and that you're watering deeply enough to prevent the turf stress that invites these mites. Banks grass mite differs from other turf mites in that it's smaller, lighter colored (green during most of the season), and adapts to warm weather, being active throughout most of the growing season rather than just cool seasons. Pesticide control is difficult, but turfgrass products designed for mite control can help. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can audit your sprinkler system to ensure even coverage and prevent the drought stress conditions that these mites exploit. Reach out at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Is buffalograss a good low-water lawn option for Denver?
response: Buffalograss is a native Colorado grass that can survive without irrigation once established, making it an excellent low-water option for Denver homeowners. It spreads by stolons (aboveground stems) that root where they touch the ground, forming a dense sod. However, there are trade-offs to consider. Buffalograss is a warm-season grass that doesn't green up until mid to late spring (well after bluegrass) and goes dormant with the first hard frost in fall, giving it a longer brown period. Non-irrigated buffalograss goes dormant during most summers and is prone to weed invasion while dormant. Establishment from seed can be labor-intensive and somewhat frustrating. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you decide if buffalograss is right for your landscape and set up minimal irrigation to keep it green. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: When does buffalograss green up and go dormant in Denver?
response: Buffalograss is a warm-season grass, so its active growing season runs from late May through early September. It breaks dormancy in mid to late spring, noticeably later than cool-season grasses like bluegrass and fescue. It turns brown with the first hard frost in fall, typically in October in the Denver area. This longer dormant period is the main drawback for homeowners used to bluegrass lawns. However, newer turf-type cultivars are more attractive and more readily available as seed, sod, and plugs. With minimal irrigation of one to two inches every two to four weeks during summer, you can keep buffalograss green and actively growing. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can configure your irrigation system for efficient buffalograss watering.

instruction: How much should I water and fertilize my buffalograss lawn?
response: Buffalograss requires surprisingly little water and fertilizer compared to bluegrass. For irrigation, provide a minimum of one to two inches of water every two to four weeks during summer to maintain active growth and an acceptably green appearance. Deeper, infrequent watering produces the best quality lawn and discourages weed invasion. You can start irrigating in mid to late May if spring is dry. For fertilization, apply no more than two pounds of total nitrogen per 1,000 square feet per growing season, split into two applications: one pound in late May to mid-June and another in late July. Over-fertilizing, especially combined with too much water, actually promotes rapid weed invasion. If your buffalograss yellows on high-pH soils, supplemental iron applications can help. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can fine-tune your system for buffalograss at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How can landscaping help reduce my energy bills in Denver?
response: Strategic landscaping can significantly reduce your home's energy costs in several ways. Vegetation along the sunny borders of your home reduces the amount of solar radiation reaching your walls, which cuts heat conduction into the house during summer. Shade the south-facing roof and wall surfaces for midday sun protection, and plant along east and west-facing walls to block morning and afternoon sun. In winter, deciduous trees drop their leaves to allow solar heating that reduces heat loss. Blocking cold winter winds with evergreen windbreaks also cuts heating costs. Because of Colorado's low humidity and cool summer nights, proper landscaping can even reduce or eliminate the need for air conditioning. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you plan and irrigate energy-saving landscape plantings. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Where should I plant shade trees around my Denver home for the best energy savings?
response: For maximum energy savings, plant deciduous shade trees in an arc encompassing your home's south, east, and west-facing walls. South-facing surfaces receive the most direct midday sun, so shading the roof and walls there provides the biggest cooling benefit in summer. East and west-facing walls receive direct sun in the morning and afternoon respectively, so trees shading those areas are also very effective. Large expanses of east or west-facing glass can admit unwanted solar radiation in summer if left unshaded. Use deciduous trees so they drop their leaves in winter, allowing sunlight through to warm your home. South-facing windows can help heat your home in winter, so avoid blocking them with evergreens. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help with tree selection and irrigation planning at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What is a sun pocket and how can I create one in my Denver yard?
response: A sun pocket is a warm, sheltered area on the south side of your home created by using fences, windbreak plantings, and shade trees together. It takes advantage of Colorado's often sunny but cool winter days by blocking cold winds while letting sunlight in, creating a microclimate several degrees warmer than exposed areas. Sun pockets make excellent locations for patios, greenhouses, or winter outdoor activity areas. To plan one, observe how the sun strikes your house between 9 a.m. and 3 p.m. in winter, and note winter wind patterns from snow drift patterns. Then position windbreaks to block wind flow while keeping the south-facing exposure open to solar heating. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you design and install the plantings for an effective sun pocket. Call us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: I have white crusty patches on my lawn. Is that salt, and what can I do about it?
response: Those white deposits are likely "white alkali" or soluble salts, which are common in many areas of Colorado. They consist of chlorides, sulfates, carbonates, and nitrates of calcium, magnesium, potassium, and other minerals. The good news is that these salts are mostly soluble and can be leached out of soils with good internal drainage and good quality irrigation water, providing a permanent solution. If drainage is poor, which is often the case, you'll need to improve it first. Add organic matter at 3 to 6 cubic yards per 1,000 square feet and mix it thoroughly with existing soil to at least 6 inches deep. Avoid excessive fresh manure since it can be high in salts itself. Don't add sand to clay soil as it often sets up like concrete. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help assess and address salt issues in your lawn. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Are crabapple trees a good fit for my small Denver yard?
response: Crabapple trees are an excellent choice for smaller Denver yards because they are small to medium-sized trees ideal for today's residential lots. They are incredibly versatile and can be used under power lines, as screens, specimen trees, patio trees, wildlife habitat, or grouped in mass plantings. Smaller or dwarf varieties can even be planted in containers. Several varieties including Dolgo, Radiant, Spring Snow, and Thunderchild are hardy to 8,000 feet elevation, so they work across the Denver metro area and into the foothills. Crabapples offer multi-season interest with spring flowers, summer fruit, and some varieties have showy fall leaf colors in yellow, orange, red, and purple. Plant them in full sun for best flowering and fruiting. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help with proper placement and irrigation setup.

instruction: What should I know about planting a crabapple tree in my Denver landscape?
response: When planting a crabapple in Denver, full sun is essential for the best flowering, fruiting, and overall health. In excessive shade, crabapples become more open, produce fewer flowers and fruit, and develop more problems with powdery mildew. There are approximately 100 varieties commonly planted nationally, with about 25 readily available in Colorado. Choose a variety based on your needs, considering mature size, growth habit, flower color, and fruit characteristics. Some crabapple fruits are valued for making jams and jellies, while others are primarily ornamental. Many develop attractive mottled bark as they mature. Some varieties are budded onto dwarfing rootstock for smaller spaces. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can advise on the best variety for your space and set up proper irrigation. Contact us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What crabapple varieties resist disease well in Colorado?
response: Disease resistance is one of the most important factors when choosing a crabapple for Colorado. The main diseases to watch for are fire blight, apple scab, cedar-apple rust, and powdery mildew. When selecting varieties, look for those rated 'R' (resistant) rather than 'S' (sensitive) for these diseases. The Thunderchild variety, with its rose-pink flowers and purple leaves in a compact 15 by 15 foot rounded form, is one popular Colorado option. Always ask your nursery about disease resistance ratings for the specific varieties they carry, as susceptibility varies significantly between the roughly 25 varieties commonly available in our area. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you choose a disease-resistant crabapple that fits your landscape. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What small trees work well in mountain communities near Denver?
response: Several small trees perform well in Colorado mountain communities. Russian hawthorn (Crataegus ambigua) grows 15 to 20 feet with showy white spring flowers, persistent red fruit, deeply lobed leaves, and yellow to orange fall color. Toba hawthorn (Crataegus x mordenensis 'Toba') is thornless with very showy fragrant double white flowers that fade to pink. Common hackberry (Celtis occidentalis) reaches 45 to 50 feet with distinctive warty bark and excellent wind and snow tolerance, and it's native to Colorado. For ornamental interest, crabapple varieties like Dolgo, Radiant, and Spring Snow all do well at elevation. At higher altitudes, expect woody plants to be somewhat smaller due to the shorter growing season. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape serves foothill communities near Denver. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What shrubs attract birds to my mountain property near Denver?
response: Several shrubs that thrive in Colorado mountain areas are excellent for attracting birds. American cranberrybush viburnum (Viburnum trilobum) reaches 6 to 8 feet with an upright arching habit, producing white flowers followed by red fruits that birds love, and its maple-like leaves turn deep red-purple in fall. European cranberrybush (Viburnum opulus) is slightly larger at 8 to 10 feet with persistent red fruit that attracts birds and many cultivars available, though it can be aphid-prone. Serviceberry produces edible blue fruits in midsummer that are very attractive to birds. These shrubs also provide cover and nesting habitat. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you create a bird-friendly landscape with proper irrigation, even at higher elevations. Contact us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Can I treat my ash tree for emerald ash borer myself?
response: CSU Extension strongly advises homeowners against purchasing imidacloprid (the most common EAB insecticide) online or from out-of-state sources. In Colorado, imidacloprid products for EAB treatment are classified as Restricted Use Pesticides, meaning they can only be sold by licensed vendors. If you want imidacloprid treatment, hire a licensed pest control operator. The treatment is most effective on small trees (less than 6 inches diameter at breast height) and is applied as a soil drench or injection around the base of the trunk. Any mulch must be removed first, and the treatment cannot be made if flowering plants are near the base of the tree, as the insecticide can move into plant nectar and harm pollinators. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can ensure your ash trees are properly watered to support treatment effectiveness. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: How does imidacloprid treatment work for emerald ash borer?
response: Imidacloprid is applied to the soil around your ash tree, either as a drench mixed with several gallons of water poured around the trunk base, or injected into the soil at multiple points under the canopy by a professional using specialized equipment. The tree's roots absorb the insecticide and transport it throughout the tree. For the treatment to work, the soil must remain moist so the roots can uptake the product. Water the treated area immediately after application and keep the soil moist for two to three weeks afterward. This is why proper irrigation is critical to treatment success. Keep in mind that imidacloprid works best on smaller trees and takes time to reach the canopy. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help maintain the soil moisture needed for effective EAB treatment. Contact us at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What should I consider when choosing evergreen shrubs for my Denver yard?
response: When selecting evergreen shrubs for your Denver landscape, several factors matter. First, understand the difference between broadleaved evergreens (like holly) and narrow-leaved types (like junipers and pines). Broadleaved evergreens generally need protection from winter sun, cold, and drying winds, and require more consistent soil moisture. Good soil drainage is essential for all evergreens. Consider the mature size when planting, as overcrowding affects their natural shape and beauty. All evergreens lose some leaves each year, and heavily sheared ones tend to be bare on the inside because the outer growth shades interior foliage. Many broadleaved evergreens struggle in Colorado if not planted in the right conditions. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you choose and properly irrigate evergreens suited to your site. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: Why is my evergreen shrub bare and brown on the inside?
response: Interior browning and bare spots on evergreen shrubs are often caused by natural leaf drop combined with shearing practices. All evergreens lose some of their older, innermost foliage each year, but shearing promotes dense outer growth that excessively shades the interior foliage, causing it to drop prematurely. This leaves the shrub looking green on the outside but bare on the inside. Narrow-leaved evergreens like pines and junipers can maintain foliage for two or more years before inner needles naturally drop. Broadleaved evergreens typically lose some older leaves during winter or when new growth resumes in spring. To maintain a fuller shrub, consider selective pruning rather than heavy shearing. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can advise on proper evergreen care and irrigation for your landscape. Contact us at info@denversprinklerservices.com.

instruction: What evergreen shrubs can I use as ground cover in my Denver landscape?
response: Several low-growing evergreen shrubs work well as ground covers in Denver landscapes. Wintercreeper euonymus (Euonymus fortunei) varieties like Emerald Gaiety, with green leaves and white margins that turn pink in winter, and Emerald 'n Gold, with green leaves and gold margins, are mounded and spreading forms that can also climb. Moonshadow euonymus spreads nicely with yellow leaves and green irregular margins. For a unique option, manzanita (Arctostaphylos x coloradensis) has a spreading habit with white-pink flowers followed by red fruit, though it needs well-drained soil. Daphne x burkwoodii, including the 'Carol Mackie' variety, offers fragrant pink spring flowers. Many of these need protected locations in Colorado's climate. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape can help you select the right evergreen ground covers for your property. Call (303) 993-8717.

instruction: What are aphids and how do I identify them on my Denver trees?
response: Aphids are small oval-shaped insects, ranging from 1.5 to 5 millimeters, that cluster on leaves, stems, and sometimes branches of trees and shrubs. They have a distinctive pair of pipe-like structures called cornicles protruding from the back of their body. Colors vary widely by species, ranging from very pale yellow to nearly black, with most being shades of green or orange. Some species are even bright red. Many different aphid species attack specific trees in Denver landscapes, including Norway maple aphids, linden aphids, elm leaf aphids, and black cherry aphids. Some aphids like the woolly beech aphid produce a fuzzy coating that obscures their body shape. If you notice sticky residue (honeydew) on surfaces under your trees, aphids are likely the cause. Contact Denver Sprinkler and Landscape for landscape health advice at (303) 993-8717.

instruction: My cherry tree has clusters of small black insects on the new growth. What are they?
response: Those are most likely black cherry aphids (Myzus cerasi), which are a common pest of cherry trees, especially tart cherries, throughout the Denver area. Aphids feed on the tender new growth of trees by sucking plant sap, and they tend to cluster on leaves and stems. While they can cause leaf curling and cosmetic damage, healthy, well-watered trees can usually tolerate moderate aphid populations. A strong spray of water from a hose can knock aphids off and is often an effective control method for small infestations. Beneficial insects like ladybugs and lacewings are natural predators that help keep aphid numbers in check. Making sure your trees are well-irrigated and healthy is the best long-term defense. Denver Sprinkler and Landscape is here to help with tree watering needs. Call us at (303) 993-8717.
